findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct all references to F3's job names from `test-wheel`/`test-sdist` to `wheel_test`/`sdist_test` (the actual IDs in release.yml lines 138 and 168) in AC 0, the retention AC, and the What section, so the grep-based dependency gate and post-landing verification match the real workflow.
evidence: Plan AC 0: 'confirm by grep that release.yml on the working base already contains F3's `test-wheel` / `test-sdist` jobs'
release.yml line 138: `wheel_test:` (not `test-wheel`)
release.yml line 168: `sdist_test:` (not `test-sdist`)
Plan 'What' section: 'retains F3's build-once → test-wheel / test-sdist → hash-gated `publish` job graph'
The AC retention check: 'verified by grep: those job names still present'
findings.finding: The plan repeatedly names F3's jobs as `test-wheel` and `test-sdist` (in both the What section and AC 0's grep instruction: 'confirm by grep that release.yml on the working base already contains F3's `test-wheel` / `test-sdist` jobs'), but the actual job IDs in the current release.yml are `wheel_test` (line 138) and `sdist_test` (line 168). AC 0 and the AC immediately after both specify grep checks against these names; a literal grep for `test-wheel` or `test-sdist` will find nothing in the current file and will incorrectly signal that F3 has not landed.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section ('F3's build-once → test-wheel / test-sdist') and AC 0 ('F3's `test-wheel` / `test-sdist` jobs')
scenarios: AC 0's pre-flight grep check runs, finds no `test-wheel` or `test-sdist` job names, and incorrectly concludes F3 has not landed — causing the implementor to stop and rebase when F3 is already present under its actual names.
The post-implementation AC verification grep returns a false pass/fail depending on whether the implementor preserved the underscore or hyphen naming convention.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC 0, change 'F3's `test-wheel` / `test-sdist` jobs' to 'F3's `wheel_test` / `sdist_test` jobs'. In the What/AC sections that say 'test-wheel / test-sdist', replace with 'wheel_test / sdist_test' throughout.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py` to the scope/edit-target list and enumerate which of its invariants (test_single_annotated_zizmor_suppression, test_g_publish_hash_gated_no_rebuild, any tag-context `if:` structural checks) must be updated when the trigger and job guards in release.yml are re-keyed.
evidence: Plan scope lists: .github/workflows/release.yml, docs/releasing.md, unit tests for NEW guard helpers — but never names tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py
test_single_annotated_zizmor_suppression (line 113-124): asserts `ignores == ['excessive-permissions']` — exactly one suppression allowed
release.yml line 324: current single suppression is `# zizmor:ignore[excessive-permissions]` on mcp_registry
Plan adds a new OIDC-privileged job (preflight/dispatch) with `id-token: write` — zizmor will flag it, potentially requiring a new suppression that would break the 'exactly one' assertion

Plan removes `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` from publish/mcp_registry/github_release — these conditions are tested by test_g_publish_hash_gated_no_rebuild and structurally in test_mcp_verify_registry_split
findings.finding: tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py is missing from the plan's scope/edit-target list, but the plan's changes to release.yml will break multiple invariants asserted there: (a) test_single_annotated_zizmor_suppression asserts EXACTLY ONE zizmor:ignore[excessive-permissions] comment; adding a new OIDC-capable preflight job or touching the publish job's permissions may require a second suppression, which would fail that assertion; (b) the three `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` guards targeted for removal are structurally tested in tests for the mcp_registry and publish jobs; (c) test_g_publish_hash_gated_no_rebuild tests the publish job's guard expression. None of these are acknowledged as needing updates.
findings.location: Scope section / tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py
scenarios: Implementation adds the preflight job with id-token: write and adds a zizmor suppression comment — test_single_annotated_zizmor_suppression fails because ignores now has two entries
Implementation does NOT add suppression — make lint fails because zizmor flags the new id-token: write job
Either path breaks make test or make lint — the Gerrit Verified gate blocks landing
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: '`tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py` — update test_single_annotated_zizmor_suppression to account for any new/removed zizmor suppressions; update test_g_publish_hash_gated_no_rebuild and any job-guard structural checks whose regex patterns reference the tag-push `if:` expression that is being replaced.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the tag-creation ordering gap: specify whether `github_release` drops `--verify-tag` (letting `gh release create` create the tag directly), or adds a prior explicit `git tag + git push` step (requiring `contents: write` permission on main — which conflicts with the branch ruleset), or uses `gh api /repos/{owner}/{repo}/git/tags` + `gh api /repos/{owner}/{repo}/git/refs` to create an annotated tag before `gh release create --verify-tag`.
evidence: Plan states: 'the workflow creates the v* tag and the GitHub Release for the record/mirror; the tag is an output, never a trigger'
Plan states: 'There is NO intermediate tag push' and 'no PAT / GitHub App secret is introduced'
release.yml:266: `gh release create "$TAG" --title "$TAG" --generate-notes --latest --verify-tag dist/*` — --verify-tag fails when tag does not pre-exist
release.yml:251: comment says '--verify-tag ensures the tag exists' — this is the current contract, but contradicts 'tag is an output' under the new model
findings.finding: The plan specifies the v* tag is an output created AFTER a successful publish ('tag is an output, never a trigger'), but does not specify how the tag is pushed into the repo before `gh release create` is called. The existing `github_release` job uses `gh release create --verify-tag`, which requires the tag to already exist in the repo at call time. Under the new model — no pre-push tag relay, tag only created post-publish — the tag is absent when `github_release` runs, causing `--verify-tag` to fail. The plan must either (a) add an explicit `git tag vX.Y.Z && git push origin vX.Y.Z` step in the release job before calling `gh release create`, or (b) drop `--verify-tag` and let `gh release create` create the lightweight tag itself, or (c) use `gh api` to create an annotated tag object prior to calling `gh release create`.
findings.location: What section — 'Tag + GitHub Release created AFTER a successful publish (record only)' bullet; release.yml:251-266 (github_release job, --verify-tag flag)
scenarios: After the plan lands, the first real dispatch-triggered release run reaches github_release, calls `gh release create vX.Y.Z --verify-tag`, gets a 'tag does not exist' error, and github_release fails — meaning a successful PyPI publish has no corresponding GitHub Release and the operator must manually create one.
On a workflow retry after a partial failure (PyPI succeeded, github_release failed), an operator re-runs from github_release — the tag still doesn't exist, so the retry also fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `gh release create "$TAG" --title "$TAG" --generate-notes --latest dist/*` (drop `--verify-tag`); `gh release create` will create a lightweight tag pointing at the workflow's SHA if the tag does not already exist. If an annotated tag is desired, add a `gh api --method POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/git/tags` + `gh api --method POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/git/refs` step before `gh release create`, using the workflow's own `GITHUB_TOKEN` (which has `contents: write` in the `github_release` job — no PAT needed, and the ruleset's `bypass_actors` includes only the deploy key, so a GITHUB_TOKEN push would be blocked). Given the ruleset lock on main branches (tags are not locked per main.tf), the lightweight-tag path via `gh release create` without `--verify-tag` is the correct approach.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the exact GitHub API JSON field paths used by the preflight decision rule (e.g. `protection_rules[].type == 'required_reviewers'` and `deployment_branch_policy` shape) with the rule description in the plan, so the implementing script and its unit-test fixture use the correct schema.
evidence: Plan states the preflight 'FAILS the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers or its deployment-branch policy is not main-only' — decision rule is present.

ABSENCE: nowhere in the plan are the specific JSON field paths of the GitHub API response co-located with this rule (e.g. which key path indicates required reviewers are set, which key path indicates branch policy is 'main'-only).
The plan also specifies a unit test for the 'env-preflight parser fails on env-protection-absent JSON' — confirming a parser exists — but the expected schema it parses is never defined in the plan.
findings.finding: The preflight decision rule ('fail if required reviewers absent or branch policy not main-only') references the GitHub Environments API response but never specifies WHICH JSON fields to inspect. The GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi response has nested fields (e.g. `protection_rules[].type == 'required_reviewers'`, `deployment_branch_policy.custom_branch_policies`) that are not co-located with the stated decision rule anywhere in the plan, leaving the implementing agent or developer to guess the exact field paths.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the pypi environment + a fail-closed preflight'; AC: 'A release-time preflight step calls GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi and fails the release closed when required reviewers are absent or the deployment-branch policy is not main-only'
scenarios: Implementing agent writes a preflight script that checks `response['protection_rules']` but the actual GH API key is `response['deployment_protection_rules']` or nested differently — the check silently passes on a structurally wrong parse.
Unit test fixture JSON does not match the real API shape because the schema was never defined — the test passes but the real step fails.
findings.suggested_fix: In the preflight bullet or a new 'Preflight schema' sub-note, add: 'The GET response is parsed for `protection_rules` containing an entry with `type == required_reviewers` and a non-empty `reviewers` list; and `deployment_branch_policy` with `custom_branch_policies == true` plus a branch-policies entry matching `main`. These are the field paths the fail-closed check must inspect and the unit-test fixtures must replicate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact GitHub `reviewers` body vocabulary for the PUT /environments/pypi step: name the specific reviewer (GitHub login or team slug), note that the API requires a numeric `id` (not a login), and state the `type` enum value ('User' or 'Team') so the step is unambiguously actionable.
evidence: Plan says: 'reviewers set to at least the repo owner / a designated maintainer' — no GitHub username, team slug, or numeric user/team ID is specified.
ABSENCE: the plan never defines the `reviewers` array object shape (GitHub API requires `[{type: 'User'|'Team', id: <integer>}]`).
The `type` enum ('User' vs 'Team') and the distinction between a login string and a numeric ID are structural vocabulary an executor must know to form the PUT body correctly.
findings.finding: The environment-configuration step requires calling PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi with a 'reviewers' body, but the `type` enum vocabulary for that field ('User' vs 'Team') and the required `id` field (numeric GitHub user/team ID, not a login) are never defined anywhere in the plan. The plan says only 'at least the repo owner / a designated maintainer' — an unresolvable value for an API call that requires a numeric ID.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The live pypi GitHub environment is configured … autonomously by the agent … it reads … if required reviewers are absent … it configures the environment via PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi (reviewers set to at least the repo owner / a designated maintainer)'
scenarios: Implementing agent passes `{type: 'User', id: 'navapbc'}` (a login string, not an integer ID) — the API returns 422 and environment configuration fails silently if the error is not caught.
Agent sets `type: 'Team'` when only individual user IDs are available, or vice versa, producing a misapplied reviewer set.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line note such as: 'Reviewer body: `[{"type": "User", "id": <numeric-github-user-id-of-repo-owner>}]`. Resolve the numeric ID via `gh api users/<login> --jq .id` before issuing the PUT. If a release-approvers team exists, use `{"type": "Team", "id": <team-id>}` instead.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the non-main test ref used in guard-fire test (i) is provisioned — either (a) document the `gh api PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/branches` or Gerrit REST step to create a transient `feature/guard-test` branch from `main` before the dispatch test, or (b) revise the test approach to trigger from `main` with a `github.ref` override the guard detects (e.g. using a different dispatch input path), acknowledging the ref-guard is separately covered by the codebase-verifiable `if: github.ref != 'refs/heads/main'` check.
evidence: Plan: 'the agent triggers release.yml on navapbc/rebar via gh workflow run with (i) a non-main ref'
infra/gerrit/replication.config:78: `push = refs/heads/feature/*:refs/heads/feature/*` — only feature/* branches replicate; they are transient
infra/terraform-github/main.tf:71: mirror lock covers only refs/heads/main; feature branches are not locked but require Gerrit to create
gh workflow run <workflow> --ref <ref> requires the ref to exist in the repository (GitHub API 422 if absent)
Evidence class: Partially-verified (gh workflow run --ref flag exists and is used; but the topology constraint on creating a non-main ref with the workflow file is unaddressed)
findings.finding: The autonomous guard-fire test (i) — 'triggers `release.yml` via `gh workflow run` with a non-`main` ref' — requires a non-main branch that (a) exists in the GitHub mirror at the time the agent runs the test, and (b) carries the updated workflow file. In this mirror topology, non-main branches arrive only via Gerrit replication of `feature/*` refs (replication.config:78), which are transient. After `main` is the only branch, `gh workflow run release.yml --ref refs/heads/<anything-not-main>` will receive a 422 from the GitHub API ('ref does not exist'). The plan says this 'needs only the environment's `workflow` scope' but does not specify how the non-main test ref is provisioned.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The guards are demonstrated to fire in a real workflow_dispatch run…the agent triggers release.yml via gh workflow run with (i) a non-main ref'
scenarios: Agent calls `gh workflow run release.yml --ref refs/heads/feature/test-guard`; the branch doesn't exist; GitHub returns 422 Unprocessable Entity; the autonomous test fails before the guard even fires.
Agent falls back to testing only the version-mismatch guard (which works from main), leaving the ref-guard untested autonomously as specified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit git-tag-push step (or `gh api` tag-creation call) in the `github_release` job BEFORE calling `gh release create --verify-tag`, and re-key `TAG` from `github.ref_name` to the `workflow_dispatch` version input — specify this mechanism in the Scope section.
evidence: Plan: 'the workflow creates the v* tag and the GitHub Release for the record/mirror; the tag is an output, never a trigger'
release.yml:260: `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}` — in a dispatch run this resolves to 'main', not a version string
release.yml:266: `gh release create "$TAG" --title "$TAG" --generate-notes --latest --verify-tag dist/*` — --verify-tag requires the git tag to pre-exist

Plan says to 're-key every downstream version source and job guard from github.ref_name / the tag-context if: to the workflow_dispatch input' — but does not say 'add a git-tag-push step before gh release create'
Evidence class: Verified (observed in release.yml lines 260, 266)
findings.finding: The plan says the `github_release` job 'creates the v* tag and the GitHub Release' post-publish, but the existing job derives its tag name from `github.ref_name` (line 260 in release.yml), which in a `workflow_dispatch` run evaluates to `main` — not a version tag. The plan re-keys version sources to the dispatch input but doesn't specify a mechanism that (a) pushes the git tag object before calling `gh release create` and (b) passes the version-input-derived tag string as `TAG` (rather than `github.ref_name`). Without both, `gh release create --verify-tag` will either use `TAG=main` (wrong release name) or fail because the `vX.Y.Z` tag doesn't yet exist as a git ref in the repo.
findings.location: What section — 'Tag + GitHub Release created AFTER a successful publish (record only)'; release.yml github_release job (lines 235-267, `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}`, `gh release create "$TAG" --verify-tag`)
scenarios: First dispatch-triggered release run reaches github_release, calls `gh release create main --verify-tag`, and either creates a GitHub Release mis-titled 'main' or fails entirely — PyPI already published, no GitHub Release created.
Even if TAG is correctly re-keyed to the dispatch input version, `--verify-tag` will fail because no one has yet pushed the annotated git tag object to the repo at that point in the workflow.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `github_release` job, add a checkout step (with `fetch-depth: 0`, `persist-credentials: true`, and `contents: write` already granted) and a step: `git tag -a "v${{ inputs.version }}" -m "nava-rebar ${{ inputs.version }}" && git push origin "v${{ inputs.version }}"` — then set `TAG: v${{ inputs.version }}` instead of `github.ref_name`. Document this in the Scope section's release.yml bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add unit-test fixtures and specified behavior for env-preflight API error paths (non-200 HTTP responses: 403, 429, 5xx, timeout) — assert that each causes the step to exit non-zero (fail-closed), not silently proceed; reference these in the AC alongside the existing 'env-protection-absent JSON' fixture.
evidence: Plan specifies only one failure fixture for the env-preflight unit test: 'env-protection present/absent JSON' — no HTTP-error fixture mentioned.
Plan states 'fail-closed' intent but does not specify what the preflight step does on a non-200 HTTP response (timeout, 403, 429, 500).
The preflight step calls a live external API endpoint; a misconfigured token, GitHub outage, or rate-limit would produce a non-JSON or non-200 response not covered by the existing test spec.
findings.finding: The env-preflight step introduces a new external HTTP dependency (GitHub REST API), but the unit tests only cover the JSON-parsing happy path ('env-protection present/absent JSON') and say nothing about API failure modes — network timeout, 403 Forbidden, 429 rate-limit, or 5xx server error. The plan states the step 'fails the release closed' on a missing-reviewers JSON response, but the behavior on a non-200 HTTP response is unspecified and untested. A fail-open on API error would silently bypass the entire preflight gate.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the pypi environment + a fail-closed preflight'; AC: 'A release-time preflight step calls GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi and fails the release closed when required reviewers are absent or the deployment-branch policy is not main-only'; Unit-test AC: 'the env-preflight parser fails on env-protection-absent JSON'
scenarios: GitHub API rate-limit returns 429: preflight step might swallow the error and proceed to publish (fail-open), defeating the 'fail-closed' guarantee.
A scoped token lacks environment read permission (403): preflight silently passes instead of aborting.
Transient network timeout: ambiguous whether the step exits non-zero (correct) or times out gracefully (incorrect).
findings.suggested_fix: In the unit-test AC and the preflight-step specification, add: 'the env-preflight helper exits non-zero (fails closed) on any non-200 HTTP response or network error — verified by fixtures for 403, 429, 500, and timeout scenarios.' In the implementation, wrap the API call in explicit non-200 handling that treats any unexpected status as a hard failure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the preflight script treats any non-2xx HTTP response as a hard release-abort (fail-closed), and add a corresponding unit-test fixture (mock HTTP error response) to the unit-test AC so this behavior is codebase-verifiable.
evidence: Plan specifies only one failure fixture for the env-preflight unit test: 'env-protection present/absent JSON' — no HTTP-error fixture.
Plan states 'needs only the workflow's own read token' — a token with insufficient scope returns 403, not a JSON body with reviewers absent.
ABSENCE: no fallback or error-handling rule for non-2xx responses is specified anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The preflight step's behavior on non-2xx HTTP responses from the GitHub API is unspecified. The plan states the step 'fails the release closed' only for the case where the API returns a parseable response indicating missing reviewers, but says nothing about network errors, 403 Forbidden (insufficient token scope), 429 rate-limit, or 5xx responses — leaving the fail-open vs fail-closed decision for each error type undefined.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the pypi environment + a fail-closed preflight'; unit-test AC: 'the env-preflight parser fails on env-protection-absent JSON'
scenarios: GitHub API transiently returns 503; the preflight script exits 0 (not correctly fail-closed) and the publish proceeds without a functioning authorization gate.
Token lacks environment read permission (403); the script misinterprets the error response as 'no protection rules' rather than 'API call failed', producing an ambiguous abort message.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the preflight bullet: 'Any non-2xx response (403, 429, 5xx, timeout) from the GET is treated as an indeterminate state and fails the release closed with an explicit error message distinguishing API failure from confirmed missing protection.' Add a `test_env_preflight_fails_closed_on_http_error` fixture to the unit-test AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the preflight script treats ANY non-2xx response from `GET /environments/pypi` as a hard failure (fail-closed), and add a note in the AC clarifying that transient API errors abort the release rather than being silently skipped.
evidence: Plan specifies the step 'FAILS the release closed' for a missing-reviewers payload but is silent on HTTP errors, timeouts, and rate-limit responses from the GitHub API.
No retry/backoff or explicit error-vs-misconfiguration distinction is mentioned for the preflight API call.
findings.finding: The preflight `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` call has no specified behavior for transient GitHub API failures (network error, rate limit, 5xx). If the call itself errors (vs. returning a clean 'no required reviewers' payload), the plan does not say whether the workflow fails-closed (treats the error as missing protection) or fails-open (skips the check). A fail-open on a transient error would silently disable the only machine-enforced gate.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the pypi environment + a fail-closed preflight'; AC: 'A release-time preflight step calls GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi and fails the release closed when required reviewers are absent'
scenarios: GitHub API is transiently unavailable (503/timeout) during the preflight; the step exits with an unhandled exception that GitHub Actions treats as a step failure — this is fail-closed but for the wrong reason, producing a confusing error message that hides whether the env is actually misconfigured.
More critically: if the script uses a non-zero exit only on a definitive 'missing reviewers' JSON parse and swallows API errors, a transient 5xx could allow the release to proceed.
findings.suggested_fix: In the preflight step: check the HTTP status code first; if status != 200, exit non-zero with a message like 'GitHub API unavailable — aborting release (fail-closed)'. Only parse the JSON body and check reviewers/policy when status == 200. Document this in the AC.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact GitHub username(s) or team slug(s) to set as required reviewers on the `pypi` environment (e.g. `navapbc` org owner login or a named team), so the PUT /environments step is unambiguously actionable.
evidence: "configures the environment via `PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi` (`reviewers` set to at least the repo owner / a designated maintainer"
ABSENCE: no GitHub username, team slug, or user-ID list is named anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The environment-configuration AC requires the agent to call `PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi` with a `reviewers` list, but the plan never states which specific GitHub user(s) or team(s) to set as required reviewers — it says only 'at least the repo owner / a designated maintainer.' For a security-critical gate (the second-human-approval control), 'at least the repo owner' is too vague for an agent to act on without stopping to ask.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — live `pypi` GitHub environment AC (PUT /environments/pypi step)
scenarios: Agent implementing the environment-configuration AC cannot determine which reviewer IDs to supply to the API — it must either guess or stop and ask, defeating autonomous completion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line decision to the AC, e.g.: 'Set `reviewers` to `[{type: User, id: <navapbc-owner-user-id>}]` (or the `navapbc/release-approvers` team slug if one exists).' The exact value must be a named identity, not 'at least the repo owner.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Merge the two near-duplicate F3/F4-retention ACs into a single AC with two explicit sub-bullets: (a) structural retention verified by grep for job names, and (b) version-source re-keying verified by absence of `github.ref_name`/tag-`if:` derivation — eliminating the duplicated opening clause and making each verification step unambiguous.
evidence: AC bullet 2: 'The workflow retains F3's build-once → test-wheel/test-sdist → hash-gated `publish` job graph and F4's `mcp_verify`/`mcp_registry` split + evidence-consolidation job unchanged in structure (verified by grep: those job names still present after this story's trigger re-key)'

AC bullet 4: 'The workflow retains F3's build-once → test-wheel/test-sdist → hash-gated-`publish` job graph and F4's `mcp_verify`/`mcp_registry` split + evidence-consolidation job, and re-keys the `github_release`/`mcp_registry` version sources + guards from the tag context to the `workflow_dispatch` input'
Both bullets open with nearly identical text before diverging
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria contain two near-duplicate bullets covering F3/F4 job-graph retention. The second (unlabeled) AC states 'The workflow retains F3's build-once → test-wheel/test-sdist → hash-gated publish job graph and F4's mcp_verify/mcp_registry split + evidence-consolidation job unchanged in structure' and the fourth (unlabeled) AC opens with the identical clause before adding version-source re-keying. The overlapping opening text makes it unclear which AC owns structural retention and which owns version-source re-keying, creating potential implementer confusion about what each AC is actually testing.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section, second and fourth unlabeled AC bullets
scenarios: An implementer passes the structural-retention grep check (AC 2) but leaves github.ref_name version derivation in place, incorrectly believing AC 4 is a separate, optional requirement. Or vice versa: an implementer conflates both into one check and misses one half.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace both bullets with: '[ ] The workflow retains F3's and F4's job graph: (a) `test-wheel`, `test-sdist`, hash-gated `publish`, `mcp_verify`, `mcp_registry`, and evidence-consolidation job names all present in the final `release.yml` (grep check); and (b) no `github.ref_name` or `startsWith(github.ref, refs/tags/v)` version derivation remains in `github_release` or `mcp_registry` jobs (grep check) — confirming structural retention plus complete version-source re-key.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add recovery guidance for the partial-publish state (PyPI succeeded, post-publish steps failed): either (a) make the tag/GitHub-Release/MCP jobs idempotent on re-run (skip-if-exists logic), or (b) document the manual recovery steps explicitly in `docs/releasing.md` and the AC.
evidence: 'Once publish succeeds, the workflow creates the v* tag and the GitHub Release for the record/mirror' — no compensating action or re-run guidance for partial post-publish failure.
PyPI does not allow re-uploading a version once published; a re-run would fail at the publish step, making the tag/GitHub-Release gap unrecoverable via the normal workflow path.
findings.finding: The plan creates the `v*` tag and GitHub Release AFTER a successful PyPI publish, but specifies no recovery path if the post-publish steps (tag creation, GitHub Release creation, or MCP registry) fail after the PyPI artifact is already live. A re-run of the failed workflow will fail at the PyPI upload step (PyPI rejects re-upload of an existing version), leaving the tag/GitHub-Release in a permanently incomplete state with no documented compensating action.
findings.location: What section — 'Tag + GitHub Release created AFTER a successful publish'; Scope — 'release.yml — create the v* tag + GitHub Release after a successful publish'
scenarios: PyPI publish succeeds; the `github_release` job fails (e.g., GitHub API timeout). The release engineer attempts to re-run the workflow — it now fails at the publish step ('File already exists'). The GitHub Release and tag remain uncreated with no workflow path to create them.
MCP registry job fails after PyPI publish — same unrecoverable state for MCP registration.
findings.suggested_fix: For the tag-creation step, use `git tag -f v$VERSION ... || true` with a check for 'tag already exists' and treat it as success. For GitHub Release creation, use the `--notes` flag with `gh release create --target main` and handle the 'release already exists' error by skipping. For MCP registry, ensure the registration call is idempotent. Document these behaviors in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific GitHub secret that holds the `repo`-scoped admin token used for the `PUT /environments/pypi` call (e.g. `secrets.REPO_ADMIN_TOKEN`), so the workflow step is unambiguously implementable.
evidence: "the agent reads and, if drifted, configures the environment... via the admin GitHub API (the environment holds a `repo`-scoped admin token)"
"a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API... needs only the workflow's own read token"
ABSENCE: no secret name such as `secrets.REPO_ADMIN_TOKEN` appears anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan says the workflow's preflight (GET) step uses only 'the workflow's own read token,' but the environment-configuration step (PUT) needs a `repo`-scoped admin token it describes as 'the environment holds a `repo`-scoped admin token' — yet no secret name is ever stated. A workflow step must reference a named secret (e.g. `secrets.GITHUB_ADMIN_TOKEN`); without a declared name, an agent implementing the step must invent one.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization' bullet; Scope section — preflight step; environment-config AC
scenarios: An agent writing the preflight workflow step must reference a secret by name; inventing the name creates a mismatch with the actual secret stored in the repo, causing silent auth failures.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence to the scope or AC: 'The PUT step uses `secrets.GH_ADMIN_TOKEN` (a PAT with `repo` scope stored as a repository secret); the GET/preflight step uses `secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the tag-creation step in `release.yml` is idempotent (e.g., uses `git tag -f` or checks for existing tag before creating, treating 'already exists' as success) so that partial re-runs of post-publish jobs can recover without manual intervention.
evidence: Plan states tag is created 'for the record/mirror' after publish but does not specify idempotent tag-creation behavior.
'git tag v$VERSION' fails with 'tag already exists' on re-run — no skip-if-exists logic mentioned.
The plan's recovery model for partial failures is unspecified (see related T5b finding).
findings.finding: The `v*` tag creation step (post-publish) is not specified to be idempotent. If the workflow is partially re-run (e.g., only the post-publish jobs are retried via 'Re-run failed jobs'), the `git tag v$VERSION` command will fail because the tag already exists from the previous run, even if the intent is to recover from a failed GitHub Release or MCP step.
findings.location: What section — 'Tag + GitHub Release created AFTER a successful publish (record only)'; Scope — 'create the v* tag + GitHub Release after a successful publish'
scenarios: First run: publish succeeds, tag created, GitHub Release creation fails. Maintainer clicks 'Re-run failed jobs'. The tag-creation step now fails ('tag already exists'), preventing the GitHub Release from being created via the normal workflow path.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace bare `git tag v$VERSION` with:
```bash
git tag v$VERSION 2>/dev/null || echo "Tag v$VERSION already exists — skipping (idempotent)"
```
Similarly, for `gh release create`, add `--notes-from-tag` and handle 'already exists' exit code gracefully.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document in the plan (and in `docs/releasing.md`) the exact scope, storage location, and rotation policy of the admin GitHub token used for autonomous `pypi` environment configuration; confirm whether a narrower-scoped credential (GitHub App installation token with only `administration` permission, or a classic token with `repo:environment` if available) can replace the `repo`-scoped PAT.
evidence: Plan text: 'the environment holds a `repo`-scoped admin token'
Plan text: 'it reads GET /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi; if … it configures the environment via PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi'
Plan text: 'no new secret' is stated for the publish OIDC path, but the env-config admin token is a separate long-lived credential
findings.finding: The plan's autonomous environment-configuration AC requires a GitHub token with `repo` scope (stated as 'the environment holds a `repo`-scoped admin token') to call `PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi`. This is a long-lived credential with broad repository read/write authority stored in the `pypi` deployment environment. The plan introduces this credential without acknowledging its scope or whether a narrower scope (e.g., a GitHub App installation token) would suffice, and without discussing its rotation/lifecycle.
findings.location: Plan section: 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment' / AC: 'The live `pypi` GitHub environment is configured… autonomously by the agent using the environment's admin GitHub token'
scenarios: The `repo`-scoped PAT stored in the `pypi` environment leaks (e.g., via a log, an artifact, or a supply-chain compromise of the workflow that uses it) — it grants full read/write on the repository, far beyond what environment-settings management requires.
The PAT expires or is rotated without updating the `pypi` environment secret, silently breaking the autonomous configuration step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan covering both the workflow trigger change (Gerrit revert procedure + required CI cycle) and the pypi environment re-key (document how to restore the v*-tag or dispatch policy via admin API), so the team has a tested recovery path if the new dispatch publish path fails after landing.
evidence: 're-key every downstream version source and job guard from github.ref_name / the tag-context if: to the workflow_dispatch input'
'environment's deployment-ref policy is re-keyed from v* tags only to main via workflow_dispatch with required reviewers'
'if the admin API write is denied (request repo-admin on navapbc/rebar)' — only escalation path mentioned, not a rollback
No 'rollback', 'revert', 'back-out', or 'recover' language found anywhere in the plan for either the trigger removal or the environment re-key
findings.finding: The plan removes the `push: tags: v*` trigger and re-keys the `pypi` GitHub environment from 'v* tags only' to 'main via workflow_dispatch' — both destructive/irreversible operations — but provides no rollback or back-out plan for either. The workflow-file change can be reverted via Gerrit, but the external environment configuration (set via admin API) is a stateful side-effect with no documented recovery procedure. If the new dispatch path proves broken post-landing (e.g., environment policy misconfigured, OIDC mismatch, preflight loop), there is no described path to restore a working release capability without escalation.
findings.location: What section ('re-key the pypi environment'), Scope section ('re-keyed from v* tags only to main via workflow_dispatch'), AC for live pypi environment configuration
scenarios: The re-keyed environment has a configuration error and the preflight step fails on every dispatch run, blocking all releases. The team needs to ship a hotfix immediately. There is no documented procedure to temporarily restore the tag-based path or reset the environment to a known-good state.
The OIDC token subject claim mismatch is discovered after the environment is re-keyed but before a successful dispatch publish — both paths are now broken and no rollback plan exists.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection in Scope: (1) Workflow trigger: revert the release.yml commit via Gerrit and land through the two-vote gate — note the expected CI cycle time. (2) Environment re-key: document the PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi payload that restores the tag-based deployment-branch policy, and identify who holds the admin token needed to execute it under incident conditions. Consider scripting both as a runbook step in docs/releasing.md so recovery is one command, not improvised.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the two-call sequence explicit in the AC: specify that after `PUT .../environments/pypi` sets `custom_branch_policies: true`, a separate `POST .../environments/pypi/deployment-branch-policies {name: 'main'}` is required to create the branch entry, and that the re-read confirmation checks BOTH the policy type AND the presence of the 'main' branch entry.
evidence: Plan: 'configures the environment via PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi (reviewers set…; deployment_branch_policy = custom + a main-only branch policy via the deployment-branch-policies endpoint)'
GitHub REST API: PUT /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/{environment_name} sets deployment_branch_policy type to custom but does NOT create branch entries
GitHub REST API: POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/{environment_name}/deployment-branch-policies creates individual branch name patterns
Evidence class: Partially-verified (API structure is known from GitHub docs; not live-probed, but the two-step requirement is the documented contract)
findings.finding: The plan describes configuring the `pypi` environment's `main`-only deployment-branch policy via 'PUT /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi' and 'the deployment-branch-policies endpoint' as if this is a single-step operation. In reality, the GitHub Environments REST API requires two sequential calls: (1) `PUT /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` with `deployment_branch_policy: {protected_branches: false, custom_branch_policies: true}` to enable custom policies, then (2) `POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi/deployment-branch-policies` with `{name: 'main'}` to add the branch pattern. The `PUT` alone does not create any branch entries; without the POST, the environment has 'custom' policy mode but zero allowed branches — blocking ALL deploys including legitimate main-dispatched ones.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the pypi environment' bullet; Acceptance Criteria — 'The live pypi GitHub environment is configured…autonomously…PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi…deployment-branch-policies endpoint'
scenarios: Agent calls only PUT with deployment_branch_policy: {custom_branch_policies: true} and considers it done; the live environment now has custom mode with no allowed branches; the release-time preflight might parse this as 'main-only policy present' (if checking only the policy type, not the entries), while actual deploys are blocked for all refs including main.
Autonomous re-read after the PUT shows deployment_branch_policy.custom_branch_policies=true and no further check — confirming a configuration that actually blocks all deployment.
findings.suggested_fix: In the environment-config AC, replace 'deployment_branch_policy = custom + a main-only branch policy via the deployment-branch-policies endpoint' with an explicit two-step: step 1 `PUT .../environments/pypi` body `{..., "deployment_branch_policy": {"protected_branches": false, "custom_branch_policies": true}}`, step 2 `POST .../environments/pypi/deployment-branch-policies` body `{"name": "main"}`. Confirm by re-reading and checking that `deployment_branch_policy.custom_branch_policies == true` AND the branch-policies list contains exactly `{name: 'main'}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `concurrency:` group to `release.yml` (e.g., `group: release-${{ github.event.inputs.version }}`, `cancel-in-progress: false`) so that at most one release workflow runs at a time per version, preventing race conditions between simultaneous dispatches.
evidence: No `concurrency:` group is specified for the release workflow in the plan.
GitHub Actions allows multiple simultaneous workflow_dispatch runs of the same workflow by default.
Both runs would independently pass the preflight (both read the same compliant env config) and proceed to the publish job.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no workflow-level concurrency control. Two simultaneous `workflow_dispatch` triggers (e.g., two maintainers independently initiating a release, or a double-click) could both pass the preflight and environment-reviewer gates independently and attempt to publish simultaneously. The second PyPI upload would fail with a 400 ('File already exists'), but both workflows might have already proceeded past the ancestry/version checks and be mid-flight — potentially creating duplicate tags, duplicate GitHub Releases, or partial MCP registrations.
findings.location: Scope section — `.github/workflows/release.yml — switch the trigger to workflow_dispatch`; AC: workflow_dispatch trigger with version input
scenarios: Two maintainers independently trigger `gh workflow run release.yml` with the same version within seconds. Both pass all guards. The first publish succeeds; the second fails at PyPI upload. The second run may have already created or begun creating the GitHub Release/tag, resulting in a conflicting or duplicate state.
A single maintainer accidentally double-triggers the dispatch; the second run races the first.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to `release.yml`:
```yaml
concurrency:
  group: release-${{ github.event.inputs.version || 'default' }}
  cancel-in-progress: false
```
This serializes releases for the same version and surfaces the conflict immediately rather than letting both proceed to a partial-failure state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the `release.yml` line number citations in the 'Why' section from `(release.yml:53/81/116)` to the current actual lines `(release.yml:205/241/323)` where the `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` guards appear.
evidence: Plan text: '(release.yml:53/81/116)'
Actual release.yml line 53: part of evidence-shapin upload-artifact step
Actual release.yml line 81: GITHUB_SHA env var assignment in build job
Actual release.yml line 116: upload-artifact for evidence-build
Actual `if:` guard lines: 205, 241, 323
findings.finding: The plan's 'Why' section cites `release.yml:53/81/116` as the line numbers for the `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` guards, but a read of the current release.yml shows those conditions are at lines 205, 241, and 323. These stale line numbers appear in the primary premise paragraph used to justify the entire story ('The only gate anywhere is `if: …` (release.yml:53/81/116)').
findings.location: Plan 'Why' section: 'The only gate anywhere is `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` (release.yml:53/81/116)'
scenarios: An implementor verifying the stated problem by jumping to line 53/81/116 sees unrelated workflow content and cannot confirm the cited motivation, eroding confidence in the premise.
A future reviewer cross-referencing the plan's line citations to validate 'Readiness Finding 1 — verified VALID' cannot reproduce the verification.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(release.yml:53/81/116)' with '(release.yml:205/241/323)' in the Why section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the docstring in `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` to replace the now-false rationale ('tags MUST stay human-pushable so release.yml can publish on a v* tag push') with the post-story truth (tags remain pushable for record/tooling purposes; publication is via dispatch-from-main, not tag push).
evidence: test_mirror_lock_terraform.py line 6-7: '- reintroduce a tag lock (tags MUST stay human-pushable so .github/workflows/release.yml can publish to PyPI on a v* tag push)'
Plan: 'With the environment keyed to dispatch-from-main, a hand-pushed v* tag cannot reach the pypi environment and therefore cannot publish'
Plan: 'infra/terraform-github/main.tf and its no-tag-lock contract test remain UNCHANGED'
findings.finding: tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py's docstring says 'tags MUST stay human-pushable so release.yml can publish to PyPI on a v* tag push' — but after this story lands, a v* tag push can NO LONGER publish (the pypi environment is re-keyed to main dispatch only). The plan says the contract test 'remains unchanged, deliberate' — the assertion itself (no tag lock) remains correct, but the stated rationale in the test docstring becomes a false statement that will mislead future editors about why tags must be pushable.
findings.location: Scope section ('main.tf / tag-push restriction stays OUT of scope') / tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py lines 6-7
scenarios: A future developer reads the test docstring, concludes 'tags must stay pushable to trigger PyPI publish', and is confused why there is no tag-triggered publish path in the workflow
A future story to add a tag-push bypass is incorrectly scoped as 'safe' because it restores the documented behavior
findings.suggested_fix: Change the docstring bullet to: '- reintroduce a tag lock (tags must remain pushable — the workflow creates a v* tag as an output record after publish; the *publication trigger* is dispatch-from-main, not the tag push)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add a TestPyPI dry-run AC that exercises the full dispatch-from-`main` → OIDC-token-exchange → trusted-publisher-accept path without publishing to production PyPI, OR (b) explicitly tag the positive-path verification as `[operator-attested]` and name the first real release as the attestation event, so the gap is a documented and tracked acceptance rather than an invisible assumption.
evidence: "The positive clean-dispatch → real-PyPI publish is deliberately NOT triggered (publishing a real release is out of scope for verifying the story; the guard-PASS path is covered by the unit-tested helpers + the codebase-verifiable dispatch-from-main wiring)"
"A staging fork + a TestPyPI trusted-publisher would only be needed to exercise the positive real-publish path, which is deliberately NOT part of verifying this story"
ABSENCE: no named child story or ticket is cited to own the positive-path E2E verification
findings.finding: The new dispatch-from-`main` → live PyPI trusted-publishing path is a cutover to a new code path (replacing the tag-push trigger), but no AC exercises this path end-to-end against the real PyPI trusted publisher. The plan explicitly excludes a real publish and does not defer to a named child story — it asserts unit tests + codebase-verifiable wiring are sufficient. Per E6(f), green offline tests plus a signed completion verdict are necessary but not sufficient when the new path has a live/external boundary.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — guard-firing AC; 'Guard-firing is verified AUTONOMOUSLY' bullet in What section
scenarios: The OIDC subject / trusted-publisher match may fail on the first real dispatch run if, e.g., the PyPI entry has a ref constraint the plan assumed absent, or the environment name changed — this would surface only on the first real release, not during story acceptance.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'A test dispatch run from `main` against TestPyPI (a pre-configured TestPyPI trusted publisher entry for `release.yml` + `pypi` environment) completes the OIDC exchange successfully and the `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish` step exits 0 — confirming the trusted-publisher match fields (workflow filename, environment name) are correct in the live dispatch context.' This is a one-time setup (TestPyPI entry + one test run) and produces a concrete log artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or a `docs/adr/` entry (or a clearly marked section in CLAUDE.md / a design doc) capturing: (a) why `workflow_dispatch`-from-`main` replaces tag-push, (b) the threat model the preflight step mitigates, and (c) the deliberate out-of-scope decision to leave `main.tf` tag restrictions unchanged — so the rationale is codebase-readable, not ticket-only.
evidence: Scope lists only: '`docs/releasing.md` — document the dispatch-from-`main` process, the version-lockstep invariant, the environment re-key, and the reviewer requirement.'
No mention of an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc for the security-architecture decision.
Plan's 'Why' section captures the threat model and rationale only in the ticket body, not in a durable codebase artifact.
T5e criterion: 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / CLAUDE.md / design-doc update.'
T7 criterion: 'A new pattern/contract/config/CLI gets a doc/ADR.'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel security-architecture decision — dispatch-from-main as the release trigger, a runtime preflight that enforces environment policy, and the deliberate choice to keep main.tf tag restrictions out of scope — but scopes documentation only to `docs/releasing.md` (a how-to runbook). No ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update is planned to preserve the rationale (why dispatch-from-main instead of tag-lock, why preflight-at-release-time instead of terraform-drift coupling, the threat model behind the controls). When the ticket is closed, the decision record lives only in the issue, not in the codebase.
findings.location: Scope section / docs/releasing.md bullet; no ADR or CLAUDE.md bullet present
scenarios: A future maintainer asks why the tag-push path was removed and why main.tf was left unchanged — the only answer is a closed ticket, not a codebase-readable decision record.
A security reviewer auditing the repo finds the preflight step in release.yml but no document explaining the threat model it mitigates or the deliberate out-of-scope choices.
If docs/releasing.md is later reorganized or the runbook rewritten, the architectural rationale (dispatch-from-main threat model, preflight-vs-terraform tradeoff) is silently lost.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0NNN-release-source-authorization.md` covering: threat (arbitrary-tag publish surface), decision (dispatch-from-main + re-keyed pypi env + preflight), alternatives considered (tag-lock in main.tf, terraform-drift coupling), and consequences. Reference it from `docs/releasing.md`. This satisfies both T7(a) and T5e(c) without bloating the runbook.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit format-validation step for the `workflow_dispatch` version input (e.g. a regex asserting PEP 440 / semver conformance) as the very first guard in the dispatch flow, BEFORE the lockstep check, so an invalid format fails immediately with a clear error rather than flowing into build/publish/tag.
evidence: Plan states the lockstep check is: 'version_input == project.version == server.json.version == each server.json.packages[].version' — this catches MISMATCHES but not FORMAT invalidity when the input matches an already-malformed file version.
'creates the v* tag' — no guard on the format of the version string before it is used as a tag name.
No AC or plan bullet specifies format validation (e.g. PEP 440 / semver regex) of the dispatch input itself before build proceeds.
findings.finding: The `workflow_dispatch` version input has no stated format/schema validation before it is used to create the `v*` tag, the GitHub Release title, and the MCP-registry version field. A correctly-typed but non-semver value (e.g. 'abc', '1.0', '1.0.0-') that happens to match the version already set in the manifest files would pass the lockstep check and flow into tag creation, producing a malformed tag name and a malformed MCP/GitHub-Release version. The non-happy-path for a malformed version input is unhandled.
findings.location: Scope section / AC for version-lockstep + 'Tag + GitHub Release created AFTER a successful publish' bullet
scenarios: A maintainer types '1.0' (not valid semver/PEP 440) when the pyproject.toml already says '1.0' — lockstep passes, a malformed tag 'v1.0' is created and published to PyPI and MCP-registry.

An empty version input (if GitHub's dispatch UI allows it) would fail lockstep only if the manifest versions are non-empty — the error message would blame a 'version mismatch' rather than 'required input missing', confusing the operator.
A pre-release suffix typo such as '1.2.0.rc1' (not PEP 440 '1.2.0rc1') passes lockstep if files already contain the same typo, producing a PyPI upload that pip cannot resolve.
findings.suggested_fix:
In the first guard job, before the lockstep check, add a step such as:
```bash
if ! echo "${{ inputs.version }}" | grep -Pq '^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+(\.(a|b|rc)[0-9]+)?(\.post[0-9]+)?(\.dev[0-9]+)?$'; then
 echo "ERROR: version input '${{ inputs.version }}' is not a valid PEP 440 version."
 exit 1
fi
```
Also add a fixture in the unit tests for the guard helpers covering a malformed-format input that matches file versions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/migrations.md` (authorization-gate section: tag→dispatch migration guidance) to the leaf's Scope and file_impact, or explicitly delegate it to another child story and update that story's scope, so the parent AC requiring both `docs/releasing.md` and `docs/migrations.md` to cover the release-authorization gate is owned by exactly one story.
evidence: Parent AC (2610-9557-c3c1-460c): "`docs/releasing.md` and `docs/migrations.md` are updated to describe the new release-authorization gate, the build-once artifact flow, and the store compatibility record (including the absent-record upgrade/rollback path)."

Leaf Scope section lists only `docs/releasing.md` under documentation: "docs/releasing.md — document the dispatch-from-main process, the version-lockstep invariant, the environment re-key, and the reviewer requirement."
Leaf file_impact (from ticket data): only `.github/workflows/release.yml` and `docs/releasing.md` — `docs/migrations.md` is absent.
No other child story (F3/F4/F2) claims ownership of the authorization-gate section in `docs/migrations.md`; F2 owns the store-compat section, F3 owns build-once, F4 owns pinning.
findings.finding: The leaf's Scope and file_impact omit `docs/migrations.md` entirely, but the parent epic's AC explicitly requires both `docs/releasing.md` AND `docs/migrations.md` to be updated to describe the new release-authorization gate (among other topics). The leaf owns the release-authorization gate documentation, yet never touches `docs/migrations.md`, leaving that parent AC deliverable unowned for the authorization gate content.
findings.location: Leaf Scope section / file_impact list; parent AC: "`docs/releasing.md` and `docs/migrations.md` are updated to describe the new release-authorization gate, the build-once artifact flow, and the store compatibility record"
scenarios: At epic close, the parent AC check finds `docs/migrations.md` was never updated to cover the authorization gate, causing the epic to fail its own AC even though all five child stories are closed.
A future operator looking for migration guidance on the tag→dispatch transition cannot find it in `docs/migrations.md`, defeating the parent's intent to consolidate migration documentation.
findings.suggested_fix: In the leaf's Scope section, add a bullet: "`docs/migrations.md` — add a "Release authorization" section documenting the migration from tag-triggered publishing to `workflow_dispatch`-from-`main`, so users upgrading from a pre-dispatch release process understand the change." Also add `docs/migrations.md` to the leaf's file_impact list and a corresponding AC line.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before switching the trigger, verify the live PyPI trusted-publisher entry for `nava-rebar` has NO ref/tag constraint (inspect `https://pypi.org/manage/project/nava-rebar/settings/publishing/` or via the PyPI API), and add an AC requiring this check or updating the entry if a constraint is found.
evidence: Plan: 'PyPA Trusted Publishing matches on owner/repo + workflow filename (release.yml) + environment name (pypi) and does NOT constrain on the git ref/tag, so the tag→dispatch trigger change alters none of the matched fields'

docs/releasing.md:24: 'PyPI pending publisher is registered: project nava-rebar, repo navapbc/rebar, workflow release.yml, environment pypi' — no mention of whether a ref constraint was set at registration time
PyPI's trusted publisher registration UI offers an optional 'Tag environment' / 'Workflow ref' field — if set, the OIDC sub claim must match that pattern

The plan's only fallback is 'a mismatch would fail loudly at pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish on the first dispatch run' — but this occurs AFTER the irreversible trigger switch, meaning the first real release attempt would fail with no easy rollback of the PyPI config
findings.finding: The plan asserts the existing PyPI trusted-publisher entry is 'unaffected by the trigger change' because it matches on 'owner/repo + workflow filename + environment name' and 'does NOT constrain on the git ref/tag' — but this is only true if the entry was registered WITHOUT an optional ref constraint. PyPI trusted publishers allow an optional tag/branch pattern field; if the current entry was configured with 'refs/tags/v*' (matching the current tag-push trigger), switching to dispatch-from-main would break it. The plan does not include a step to inspect or verify the live PyPI publisher entry's configuration before making the irreversible trigger change.
findings.location: What section: 'OIDC subject stays environment-scoped — the PyPI trusted-publisher entry is unaffected by the trigger change'
scenarios: If the PyPI publisher entry was registered with a ref constraint (e.g. 'refs/tags/v*'), the first dispatch-triggered release attempt would fail at the publish step with a 401/403 OIDC mismatch — forcing a PyPI admin fix mid-release under time pressure
The plan's self-described 'loudly' failure is a silent assumption that the existing entry has NO ref constraint — not verified against the live PyPI config
findings.suggested_fix: Add a scope item: 'Confirm (and if necessary update) the PyPI trusted-publisher entry has no ref constraint — verify the entry at pypi.org/manage/project/nava-rebar/settings/publishing/ lists Environment: pypi with no Tag or ref field set.' Record this as a pre-implementation check in AC 0 or a new AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either extend `terraform-drift.yml`'s `paths` filter to also include `infra/terraform-github/**` (so the module gets the same daily plan-drift check as the AWS stack), or explicitly document in the plan/scope why this gap is acceptable and how drift for that module is otherwise detected.
evidence: terraform-drift.yml on: push: paths: ['infra/terraform/**'] — no glob covering infra/terraform-github/**
Plan states 'main.tf / tag-push restriction stays OUT of scope (unchanged, deliberate)' but does not address the absence of drift detection for that module
findings.finding: The existing `terraform-drift.yml` drift-detection workflow is scoped to `infra/terraform/**` and does NOT cover `infra/terraform-github/**`, so changes to the GitHub mirror-lock Terraform module can drift from applied reality without any automated detection. The plan leaves `infra/terraform-github/` unchanged but also does not mention extending drift detection to cover it.
findings.location: .github/workflows/terraform-drift.yml (paths filter) / infra/terraform-github/
scenarios: A manual `gh api` change to the `gerrit-mirror-lock-main` ruleset bypasses both the IaC and drift detection, and the discrepancy is never surfaced automatically.
A future `terraform apply` against `infra/terraform-github/` is made out-of-band; no CI job notices the state-vs-HCL divergence.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'infra/terraform-github/**'` to the `paths` lists in `terraform-drift.yml`'s `pull_request` and `push` triggers, and add a second `jobs:` entry (or a matrix) that runs `terraform plan` in `infra/terraform-github/` with `GITHUB_TOKEN` sourced from a repo secret — matching the daily-sweep schedule already in place for the AWS stack.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare that the admin token used for the autonomous `PUT /repos/.../environments/pypi` step is a **fine-grained PAT scoped to `administration: write`** (not a classic broad `repo` PAT), and document this in the Scope / AC for the autonomous environment-configuration step to enforce least-privilege on this boundary-crossing path.
evidence: Plan states: 'the environment holds a `repo`-scoped admin token'
Plan states the agent calls `PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi` and the deployment-branch-policies endpoint
GitHub fine-grained PATs support `administration: write` scope which is sufficient for environment management without granting code read/write
Classic `repo` PAT scope grants code read/write, issue write, PR write — all unnecessary for environment configuration
findings.finding: The autonomous environment-configuration step (AC: 'live `pypi` GitHub environment is configured…autonomously') introduces a `repo`-scoped admin token to call `PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi`, but the plan does not declare whether this is a fine-grained PAT (minimum: `administration: write`) or a classic `repo`-scope PAT. A classic `repo` PAT grants full read/write over code, issues, pull requests, and other repository data — far broader than environment administration requires — violating least-privilege on this boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The live `pypi` GitHub environment is configured…autonomously' / Scope section — 'autonomous post-landing verification'
scenarios: If the `repo`-scoped token is a classic PAT, a compromised workflow run or log leak exposes a credential that can read all repo contents, write commits, or open PRs — far beyond what the environment-admin step needs.
A future contributor implementing the autonomous AC may reach for the broadest token that 'works' (classic `repo` PAT) without realising a fine-grained token suffices.
findings.suggested_fix: In the autonomous environment-config AC, replace 'the environment's admin GitHub token' with an explicit statement: 'a fine-grained PAT with `administration: write` scope (and no code-read/write permissions) stored as an encrypted secret in the `pypi` environment; a classic `repo`-scope PAT must not be used for this step.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
